sentences.text: Yes.
To get to Wilno-- by the way, W-I-L-N-O, in Polish-- it has different names depending on the language-- you have to pass through the part of Poland occupied by Russia.
So we-- my mother, and I and an aunt and her son, we made it to a town called Bialystok in occupied Russia-- B-I-L-L-Y-S-T-O-K.
That's the same aunt, Zina Hurwicz, and her son's name is Henry.
And he's still alive and lives in California.
We had to pass an illegal border between Germany and Russia.
And there were people who conducted us, of course, for money.
And we got-- we traveled by lorry-- by, by truck-- and then we walked to some wealthy peasant's home on the German side.
No, not my father.
My father still stayed in Warsaw.
You know, the problem was to get yourself transported and whatever money you had.
And if you traveled with the money and somebody knew it, you were not likely to get anywhere.
So we traveled first.
And then my father was-- when we were already in Bialystok, my father tried to transport the money to Wilno.
Some of the money made it and got to United States.
When we got-- when the war ended, my father and I owned $8,000, which was at the time a considerable amount of money.
But anyway, so here we are in this peasant's hut-- big hut.
And then at night, we had to t-- to cross the River Bug, B-U-J, which was the border between Germany and Russia.
And we crossed it on little rowboats, got to the Russian side, walked to another peasant's home.
And when we were there, the Russian soldiers came.
And fortunately, one of them was Jewish.
And he started talking to my aunt in Yiddish.
And he said to her, tell the other soldiers that you want to go back to Germany.
And then they'll send you on to the Russian side, because they'll always do the opposite of what you want.
So he helped us.
And we did that.
And they just gathered us-- we were a group of about 10 or 12 people.
I don't remember who the others were-- put us on the train to Bialystok.
And we arrived in Bialystok where we had some relatives whose names I don't remember.
You felt frightened.
You know, it's very unfamiliar.
I'm still a child.
And here it is traipsing in the fields in the dark with a little flashlight and crossing barbed wires and traveling on the river.
And it's very frightening to a child, you know?
You know that if you could get caught, nothing good is going to happen.
But of course, I had no idea how bad things could be.
So when we were in Bialystok, we were there for several months.
And I went to school.
We lived with some relatives in an apartment.
This is also fairly large city, Bialystok-- still in Poland, by the way, now.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] some kind of a distant cousin-- I don't remember who they were at all.
I do remember that there were shortages.
I remember standing on lines to get bread, to buy bread, to buy milk, to buy different necessities of life.
And also going-- I remember going to school.
Interestingly, you know the Russians-- in Poland, high schools for the most part had to be paid for.
So only the well-to-do people could afford-- or relatively well-to-do people-- could afford to send their children to high school.
So public education ended with grammar school.
But the Russians made it available for free to anybody.
So when I went back to school, I was in a class with 18-year-old, 19-year-old, you know.
And very enterprisingly, I remember, I used to write the love letters for them-- you know, I was about 12 and a 1/2 or 13-- and for, for 50 kopeks a letter.
And I don't think I learned too much in that particular school.
But I did start to learn Russian.
No, I don't remember.
I don't remember if it had a name-- probably had a number or something like that.
Anyway, eventually, my father joined us.
And we made the effort to get to Lithuania.
And we failed.
We were traveling by truck.
And we were caught by the Russian guards on the bord-- on the border of Russia now and Lithuania.
And we were put briefly in a kind of a makeshift prison, which was a rabbi's house, as it happened.
And I think we were actually in some kind of a barn or something.
Because I remember a big space with a kind of a shelf.
And we all slept on the shelf-- the women, one next to the other.
And I do remember that mostly there were some peasant girls.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember being frightened when we were led to the bathroom-- the outhouse-- because the guard would walk behind us with a rifle at the ready, you know, like I'm going to do something.
My mother was a very, very sweet and rather weak woman, you know.
And as I'll tell you, I had to become an adult very quickly when my father wasn't with us anymore because my mother was very helpless.
She was sheltered by her father as a young woman then very much sheltered by her husband.
And one of the sweetest, most likable person you can imagine, but not a tower of strength, you know, under critical conditions in a crisis.
Anyway--
We were in that little prison.
And then we were moved to at town, I think, Grodno, G-R-O-D-N-O, to a regular prison where we were questioned.
And then my mother and I were released because the Russians said, we do not imprison children.
So they released my mother because of me.
And my father was transported to another town called Baranowicze, B-A-R-A-N-O-W-I-C-Z-E, where he was 14 months in the Russian prison.
We got to Bialystok I think the end of October of '39.
And my father joined us around April of 1940, March or April.
We were caught at the beginning of May of 1940.
And he was in prison until the war started between Germany and Russia.
In Baranowicze, in a prison.
Now meanwhile, what happened to my mother and me-- we returned to Bialystok at first.
And that was the time when the Russians were sending the Jewish refugees from the Polish part-- from the German part of Poland to Siberia.
My aunt, my uncle-- who has also joined us-- and my cousin were all sent to Siberia.
The Hurwicz.
That's how they survived.
OK?
My mother and I-- I don't remember what happened.
We had been for some reason sleeping somewhere else.
And we were not caught.
We were not gathered.
And my mother was horrified by being separated-- now that her husband wasn't with us, my father, being also separated from her brother and sister-in-law, with whom she was, by the way, very close.
And so she even went to the authorities and told them, well, we are staying in this such and such a location.
We would like to be together with my-- with my brother and sister-in-law.
And it just never happened.
So we are now there alone.
And the deportations stopped because these things go in waves.
And we were offered a sort of second class Russian citizenship that was called, I think, Passport with Clause 11.
That means you are second class citizen.
You are not allowed to live in any county seat or larger.
And you are not allowed to live within 100 kilometers of any border, which is very restricting in this part of Poland.

But we, we-- because of that, we had to move to a smaller town called Sloniam, S-L-O-N-I-A-M, approximately the town the size of West Orange, maybe 30,000, 40,000 people-- a little town, though, not a suburb.
And we lived there for over a year.
This is when I went to a Russian school and also continued taking private French lessons.
So that was a year when life was for me semi-normal, OK?
I was a member of the Pioneers, which was like in Russia sort of like Boy Scout or Girl Scouts, but with political implications, like everything in the Soviet Union.
I was a good student.
I was what they call [RUSSIAN], meaning that I had to all A's, you know, in school.
And I learned Russian pretty well.
All my teachers were from Russia.
The instruction was in Russian.
And it was semi-normal life, except, of course, that my father was in a prison.
About 40 miles away was the town of Baranowicze where he was in prison.
My mother went there once a month to deliver a package for him.
We later found out that he got very little from that package.
But at least he got enough to know that we were all right, because he was getting something from us.
No letters were allowed.
No visits were allowed.
And this life came to an end abruptly, again, when the war started between Germany and Russia.
Sloniam was overrun within two or three days by the Germans, as was the whole eastern part of Poland and a considerable part of Russia.
My father joined us very soon.
He was released from prison, oddly enough, by the Germans.
And although it was not pre-arranged, my mother had left our location with various members of the Jewish community in Baranowicze, particularly with doctors.
And he found a Jewish doctor, found out where we were, and walked to join us.
A little funny story while he was walking-- two funny stories.
The first one, there was a movement, of course, of German troops.
Now this is not the Gestapo or the SS.
This is the Wehrmacht, the German military, moving deeper into Russia.
And my father is trudging on the side of the road.
And I have to tell you that although I was not considered to look Jewish, my father did.
He was dark.
And he, he just looked Jewish.
And here these German units are tr-- are walking by, marching by.
And one of them yells to my father in German, [GERMAN].
And just by accident, that was my father's name.
And he was of course thinking of generic, for Jews, you know, like King Solomon.
Also during that trip, he had to spend the night because he couldn't walk it in, in one day.
And there were wolves.
So he went up a tree and spent the night in the trees hiding from the wolves.
And the soldiers.
Well, the soldiers probably didn't march at night.
I don't know.
But anyway, he joined us.
And life in that little town-- the town had a considerable Jewish population, did not have any doctors who were not Jewish.
So the Germans divided the doctors, the Jewish doctors, into the those who could only treat the Jews and those who could only treat the Poles.
My father was one of the doctors who was only allowed to treat the Poles.
My father was very street-smart.
He had lived through the Russian Revolution.
He hated the Russians, as you understand from his political views, which were probably very much shaped by, by his fate, by his story doing the Russian Revolution.
And he felt that living in a small town, Jews, where we didn't have really close friends except other refugees, that we were very exposed.
He felt that we would have a better chance if we actually went back to the ghetto of Warsaw.
So the decision was made to try to make it to the ghetto of Warsaw.
My father was also very brave, as I realized later on.
And he said, you know, I'm going to go to the Gestapo and ask for a pass to go back home to Warsaw.
And he went with my mother.
Now that I think of it, as an adult, I think it was a very rash decision to leave me alone, and the two of them going to Gestapo might never have come back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother, I could understand it.
You know, at one point when we were under Russian occupation, my mother went to visit her father, who lived in Wilno.
She left me alone for a month.
I must have been about 13 at the time.
And during that time, there was the deportations of refugees and other undesirable elements.
I had heard about it beforehand and had hidden with a friend I felt was least likely to be-- to be searched for.
A Jewish friend, but a local girl, not a-- you see, I knew local people because I went to school-- whose father had been a doctor.
But the parents were divorced.
So I felt that she was not a member of the intelligentsia anymore, because the father wasn't around.
And so I survived.
I wasn't sent to Siberia by myself.
But it was very irresponsible, really, of my mother to have left a 13-year-old child for a month during the war because she wanted to visit her father.
And she didn't want to take me because I would miss school.
You know, it seems rather a wrong priority.
But anyway, my parents-- to go back to the story-- my parents went to the Gestapo.
And as they got there, there was a-- I guess you would call it today some kind of a receptionist and translator.
This was in-- well, the war between Germany and Russia started in June or July of '41.
So this was maybe sometime around August or September of '41.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So this man, this receptionist, he was a-- he was a Pole.
And he looked at my father.
And he said, Dr.
Salamon-- this is the man who turned out to have been my father's patient-- what are you doing here?
My father explained it to him.
And he said, you know, this is kind of risky.
Who ever heard of a Jew of his own volition showing up at the Gestapo?
My father said, you know, because it is so rare, it might work.
So he went-- this Pole-- went to speak to he superior, the Gestapo officer, who was so intrigued by it, he said, I will see these people.
Do you know the names of either the Pole or the Gestapo officer?
No, I don't know the names.
I've never met them.
I just know the story from my parents.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the, the interview took place, of course, at the office of the Gestapo.
And the Gestapo officer, it turned out, was a man-- I don't know, in his 50s, 40s, something like that-- was also involved in the First World War.
And this was kind of a point that he and my father had in common, their hatred of Russia.
And they had a conversation, which was apparently rather amicable.
And at the end of it, he gave my father and my mother a pass to go to Warsaw.
And my mother was so unrealistic that when she came-- when they came back, she looked at this pass.
And she said, it doesn't say 'with luggage.' So my father went back, and it was added, [GERMAN], you know, with luggage.
So now we have the pass.
We are still afraid to get on a train because the first German soldier can take the pass, and tear it, and do anything that he wants.
So my father had friends with whom he had been in prison, local people who ran a tavern, not Jewish people, who were very fond of my father.
My father was the kind of man who was absolutely the worst bigot you can imagine.
He hated every group.
He hated the Poles.
He hated the Catholics.
He hated the Germans.
But he loved people.
So he was very much-- very much liked by people, because he was-- So he loved individual--
He loved individuals, yes.
And he was-- he was very much a people's person.
He was very good in relations with the people.
In prison, he was probably the kind of man in the cell that everybody looked up to.
He was the only educated man.
And he had heard incredible stories in prison from, from people who would confess to him and who would confide in him.
Anyway--
--got to Warsaw, or got to--
Yeah, what happened was these friends of my father's who ran the tavern-- of course, in a tavern, in a bar, you get to meet all kinds of soldiers who come there.
And they had met some SS soldiers who were going by, by truck to Warsaw and agreed to transport us.
They hid us on the truck-- in the truck--
Even though we had the pass.
And they transported us to Warsaw.
You know, we were afraid of them.
But it was still-- we had to take the risk to go with them.
Yeah, better than the train.
And in fact, they stopped at one point.
And my father says, oh, that's not good.
They stopped.
There's nothing.
There's no, no toll, no checkpoint.
And it turned out they stopped to let us go into the woods, you know, to just to use the facilities of the woods.
They were being kind.
They got us to the gate of the ghetto of Warsaw.
And of course, the guards who were at the gate were absolutely amazed that anybody wanted to enter the ghetto.
People were trying to escape from the ghetto, not enter it.
So they let us in.
And then we were then in the ghetto of Warsaw.
OK.
We went first to some friends.
And then, of course, our apartment, I don't know-- I don't remember whether it was taken over.
I don't even remember whether it was livable in because there were many bombed out houses in the ghetto.
But if it was lived in, somebody else lived there by that time.
We lived in, in a former clinic that my father was co-owner of, which had been, for some reason, empty.
It was not used as a clinic.
And we lived in one room of it and used, I remember, a couple of other rooms for storage.
You could not afford to, to heat more than one the room in a dwelling, really.
We had, I remember, bought a pot-bellied stove that stood in the middle of the room.
And we used it for heating.
We used it for cooking.
On the top of it, it would be like one burner, you know, what you could cook.
Well, the food was really-- the food that the Germans distributed to the Jews in the ghetto was very meager.
And you could not survive on it.
So everything you lived on was really smuggled into the ghetto.
The ghetto was at-- although it was-- it had a wall around it, mostly running through the middle of the street.
There was some places where you just couldn't do it, because it's an old city.
So there would be places, for example, where you could have the top would be outside of the ghetto, and the lower floor would be in the ghetto.
I remember there was one drain pipe like that, rain, for rain.
And that was used to pour stuff-- pour milk, and then they would to dry it, and they would pour rice, or they would pour flour, and so on.
And there were very ingenious method of smuggling food into the ghetto.
My father was again, of course, working as a doctor.
So he was making a living of sorts.
And we were much better off than many people in the ghetto.
You have to realize, the ghetto of Warsaw housed about half a million people.
So it's-- was all crowded into a fairly small area.
So living space was at a premium.
And people who had apartments by now shared them with relatives, with friends, with-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, rented rooms and stuff like that.
Well, the name of the street where we stayed during the war was called Nalewki, N-A-L-E-W-K-I.
I do have a map of the ghetto.
But I don't know if you can see it at the moment.
Maybe I'll show it to you a little later.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] life for me again became-- it's hard to say normal.
But you know, under any circumstances, things become, when they go on like that, they become the norm for you at the time.
And you adjust, to some extent, to whatever your life is.
No.
I was-- schools, of course, were not permitted in the ghetto.
There was a lot of private instruction and group instruction going on, and many teachers, of course.
I had fallen behind because the Russian school was not as good as my school had been in, in Poland.
So to catch up, I remember for about six months, I studied privately with a couple of teachers.
And at the end of the school year-- this was now around May or June of 1942-- I went to some teachers' homes and took some what you might call final exams.
And turned out, I caught up.
And these people had records, which of course doesn't exist anymore, that I had completed something, I would say, on the order of about grades seven, eight, nine-- something like that, you know.
I remember I took Latin, and math, and Polish literature.
When you carried books, you hid them because occasionally, there would be Germans in the ghetto, not very much.
And you were not allowed to walk with school books, because you're not supposed to be in school or study.
Yes.
